threats.title: Arbitrary Token Whitelisting and Gauge Creation via Fake Genesis Pool
threats.hypothesis_description: The GenesisPoolManager's depositToken function accepts an arbitrary genesisPool address without validating that it was created by the GenesisPoolFactory. An attacker can pass a malicious contract that returns true, tricking the manager into whitelisting arbitrary tokens and creating gauges for arbitrary pairs.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a severe confused deputy vulnerability. By failing to verify the authenticity of the genesisPool contract, the manager blindly trusts the data returned by it (nativeToken, pairAddress) and executes privileged operations (whitelisting, gauge creation) on behalf of the attacker.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External call to user-provided address without factory validation, followed by privileged state changes

preconditions: Caller can execute depositToken (no access control)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing registry/factory validation for external contract address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: tokenHandler.whitelistToken and gaugeManager.createGauge are called using data from an untrusted contract

analysis_questions: Does gaugeManager.createGauge allow arbitrary pairs to be registered?
Does tokenHandler.whitelistToken allow arbitrary tokens to bypass protocol restrictions?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock malicious GenesisPool contract
Call depositToken and verify if a gauge is successfully created for an attacker-controlled pair

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys FakeGenesisPool that returns a malicious pair address and token address. Attacker calls GenesisPoolManager.depositToken(fakePool, 1). The manager whitelists the attacker's token and creates a gauge for the attacker's pair, allowing them to steal protocol emissions.
prerequisites: Ability to deploy a custom smart contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Critical - Attacker can redirect protocol emissions to their own pairs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governance intervention to blacklist the token and kill the gauge

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for invalid/unregistered genesis pool addresses in depositToken

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain via unexpected GaugeCreated events

threats.title: Defeated and Expired Governance Proposals Can Be Executed
threats.hypothesis_description: The execute() function in the Governor contract explicitly allows proposals in the Defeated or Expired states to be executed, completely bypassing the voting consensus mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement checking the proposal status uses logical OR (||) to permit execution if the status is Succeeded, Defeated, or Expired. This is a catastrophic logic error that renders governance voting meaningless, as any proposed action can be executed regardless of the vote outcome.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)

preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to create a proposal (or proposal threshold is 0)

analysis_questions: Is there any secondary validation within _execute that prevents this?
What is the proposalThreshold required to submit a malicious proposal?
validation_requirements: Create a proposal, vote against it so it becomes Defeated, and attempt to call execute()

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a proposal to transfer protocol treasury funds to themselves. The community votes it down, and its state becomes Defeated. The attacker calls execute() anyway, and the funds are transferred.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Critical - Complete protocol takeover and treasury drain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None if treasury is drained or admin roles are transferred

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing negative tests verifying that Defeated proposals revert on execute()

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block (requires waiting for voting period to end)
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via ProposalExecuted events for Defeated proposals

threats.title: Router Setter Bricked Due to Inverted Zero-Address Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The setRouter function in GenesisPoolManager contains a typo in its require statement, enforcing that the new router address MUST be address(0). This permanently bricks the ability to update the router to a valid address.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the protocol ever needs to upgrade the router, the owner will call setRouter. Due to the typo, any valid address will revert. If the owner accidentally sets it to address(0) (which is the only accepted value), all future genesis pool launches will fail because they will attempt to call addLiquidity on address(0).
preconditions: Owner attempts to update the router address

analysis_questions: Is there any alternative path to update the router?
Is the contract upgradeable to fix this typo?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call setRouter with a valid address and verify it reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol needs to migrate to a new DEX router. The owner calls setRouter(newRouter), which reverts. The protocol is permanently stuck using the old router.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High - Loss of upgradeability for core protocol infrastructure
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol admins and future genesis pool creators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires upgrading the GenesisPoolManager implementation contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for updating the router address

threats.title: Systemic Denial of Service in Epoch Flip via Unbounded Loop Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The checkAtEpochFlip function iterates over all live genesis pools and performs external calls (launching pools, adding liquidity) without a try-catch block. A single pool that reverts during launch will block the entire epoch flip transaction for all pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: An attacker can intentionally create a genesis pool with a malicious or incompatible token (e.g., a fee-on-transfer token that causes the router's addLiquidity to revert). When the epoch flips, the manager will attempt to launch this pool, revert, and prevent all other legitimate pools from launching or transitioning states.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded loop making external state-changing calls without try-catch

preconditions: One pool is designed to revert during the launch() sequence

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: DoS via failed external call in loop
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A single reverting pool bricks the entire epoch flip mechanism

analysis_questions: Can an attacker easily get a malicious token whitelisted for a genesis pool?
Is there an admin function to forcefully remove a broken pool from liveNativeTokens?
validation_requirements: Deploy a pool with a token that reverts on transfer
Trigger checkAtEpochFlip and observe if other valid pools are blocked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker gets a fee-on-transfer token approved for a genesis pool. At the epoch flip, the router's addLiquidity call reverts because it receives fewer tokens than expected. The checkAtEpochFlip transaction reverts, preventing all other genesis pools from launching.
prerequisites: Governance approval for the attacker's token (or attacker uses the Fake Genesis Pool exploit to bypass approval)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High - All genesis pool funds are temporarily or permanently locked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis pool depositors and token owners
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governance to manually reject the broken pool if possible, or upgrade the contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for epoch flip with a mix of successful and failing pools

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via repeated failing keeper transactions

threats.title: Governance Quorum Manipulation via Current Token Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: The quorum calculation in L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction uses the current total supply of smNFTs rather than the historical supply at the proposal's snapshot block. This allows the quorum requirement to be manipulated while a vote is active.
threats.investigation_rationale: By calling token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without passing the blockTimestamp, the contract evaluates quorum against the live supply. An attacker can mint new smNFTs to raise the quorum threshold (causing a passing proposal to fail) or burn smNFTs to lower it (allowing a weak malicious proposal to pass).

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses current state (no timestamp arg) for historical quorum calculation

preconditions: smNFT supply can be altered during the voting period

analysis_questions: Can smNFTs be minted or burned by users during an active voting period?
Does IBlackHoleVotes have a timestamp-based supply function available?
validation_requirements: Create a proposal, vote to exactly meet quorum, then increase total supply and verify if state() evaluates to Expired/Defeated due to missed quorum

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A legitimate proposal is about to pass and has barely met quorum. An attacker mints a large number of smNFTs just before the voting period ends. The quorum threshold increases retroactively, and the proposal fails to meet the new quorum.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High - Governance manipulation and denial of service for proposals

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying quorum stability when total supply changes during voting

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via sudden supply changes near vote deadlines

threats.title: Permanent Denial of Service for Genesis Pool Creation via Dust Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: The depositNativeToken function requires the DEX pair for the native and funding tokens to have exactly 0 balances. An attacker can preemptively create the pair and send 1 wei of the native token to it, permanently blocking the creation of a genesis pool for that token.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because the check is strictly `balanceOf(pairAddress) == 0`, any dust amount in the pair contract will cause `depositNativeToken` to revert. Since this is the only way to initialize a genesis pool, the token owner is permanently griefed and must deploy a completely new token contract to use the protocol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Strict zero-balance check on external pair contract

preconditions: Attacker knows the target native token address before the genesis pool is created

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Strict equality check on manipulable external balance
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should handle existing dust or use internal accounting

analysis_questions: Can the token owner simply use a different funding token to bypass the blocked pair?
Is there any way to withdraw the dust from the pair before initialization?
validation_requirements: Create pair manually, send 1 wei of token, and attempt to call depositNativeToken

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A project announces they will launch via Blackhole Genesis Pool. An attacker creates the Uniswap pair for the project's token and sends 1 wei to it. When the project team calls depositNativeToken, it reverts. The project cannot launch their token on Blackhole.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium - Loss of business for the protocol, forced token redeployment for projects
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Project must deploy a new token contract or use a different funding token

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for genesis pool creation when pair already exists with dust

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via direct transfers to pair contracts

threats.title: Genesis Pool Deposit Window Bypass Due to Missing Timestamp Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The depositToken function in GenesisPool does not enforce the end of the deposit window. It relies entirely on an off-chain keeper calling checkBeforeEpochFlip exactly on time. If the keeper is delayed, users can continue depositing right up to the epoch flip.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol intends to have a NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW (e.g., 3 hours) before the epoch flips where deposits are disabled, allowing voters to assess the final pool state. Because depositToken lacks a timestamp check for the end time, any delay in the keeper execution allows attackers to make last-minute deposits, gaming the allocation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing upper bound timestamp check for deposit window

preconditions: Keeper is delayed in calling checkBeforeEpochFlip

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should check block.timestamp < genesisInfo.startTime + genesisInfo.duration - NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW

analysis_questions: How reliable is the epochController keeper infrastructure?
Can an attacker intentionally delay the keeper (e.g., via gas griefing)?
validation_requirements: Simulate a delayed keeper call and verify deposits succeed within the intended quiet window

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker waits until the NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW begins. The keeper transaction is delayed due to network congestion. The attacker deposits a massive amount of funding tokens, diluting other depositors who thought the pool state was finalized.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium - Unfair allocation dilution for legitimate depositors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, allocations are finalized at epoch flip

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for deposits occurring during the intended quiet window

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via deposit events close to epoch flip

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Revert in Incentive Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The addIncentives function records the intended transfer amount rather than the actual received balance. If a fee-on-transfer token is used as an incentive, the contract will hold fewer tokens than recorded, causing transferIncentives to revert and bricking the pool's launch.
threats.investigation_rationale: When transferIncentives is called during the pre-launch phase, it attempts to approve and notify the external bribe with the recorded amount. Because the actual balance is lower due to the transfer fee, the transaction will revert. This prevents the pool from transitioning to PRE_LAUNCH, effectively bricking it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State update uses transfer parameter instead of balance difference

preconditions: Token owner adds a fee-on-transfer token as an incentive

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should calculate balance difference before and after transfer

analysis_questions: Are fee-on-transfer tokens explicitly supported or blocked by the tokenHandler?
Does this revert block the entire checkAtEpochFlip loop for all pools?
validation_requirements: Deploy a fee-on-transfer token, add it as an incentive, and verify transferIncentives reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A token owner unknowingly (or maliciously) adds a fee-on-transfer token as an incentive. At the epoch flip, the manager attempts to pre-launch the pool, but transferIncentives reverts due to insufficient balance. The pool is permanently stuck.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium - Pool is bricked, funds may be stuck until governance intervention
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Depositors in the specific genesis pool
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governance to manually reject the pool

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for fee-on-transfer tokens in addIncentives
